prompt: The Supreme Court ruling in Brown v Board of Education (1954) declared that:

options.label: A
options.text: Segregation in public transport was unconstitutional

options.label: B
options.text: Separate educational facilities were inherently unequal

options.label: C
options.text: African Americans had the right to vote without literacy tests

options.label: D
options.text: Poll taxes were unconstitutional in federal elections

markScheme: B — The ruling overturned Plessy v Ferguson (1896) and established that the 'separate but equal' doctrine in public education violated the 14th Amendment's Equal Protection Clause.

prompt: Which organisation was founded in 1960 by student activists including Diane Nash and John Lewis, following the Greensboro sit-ins?

options.label: A
options.text: Southern Christian Leadership Conference (SCLC)

options.label: B
options.text: National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP)

options.label: C
options.text: Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC)

options.label: D
options.text: Congress of Racial Equality (CORE)

markScheme: C — SNCC was formed at Shaw University in April 1960, growing out of the sit-in movement. It became increasingly radical under Stokely Carmichael from 1966 onward.

prompt: The Mukden (Manchurian) Incident of September 1931 was significant because it:

options.label: A
options.text: Marked the beginning of full-scale Sino-Japanese War

options.label: B
options.text: Provided Japan's Kwantung Army with a pretext to occupy Manchuria

options.label: C
options.text: Led directly to Japan's attack on Pearl Harbor

options.label: D
options.text: Resulted in the League of Nations imposing military sanctions on Japan

markScheme: B — Officers of the Japanese Kwantung Army staged an explosion on the South Manchurian Railway near Mukden and blamed Chinese forces, using it as justification for the military occupation of Manchuria.

prompt: The Munich Agreement of September 1938 resulted in:

options.label: A
options.text: Germany annexing Austria without military conflict

options.label: B
options.text: Britain and France formally declaring war on Germany

options.label: C
options.text: Germany being granted the Sudetenland region of Czechoslovakia

options.label: D
options.text: The Soviet Union joining a collective security arrangement with the Western powers

markScheme: C — Britain, France, Italy and Germany agreed to transfer the Sudetenland to Germany. Czechoslovakia was not invited to the negotiations. Chamberlain returned to Britain claiming 'peace in our time'.

prompt: Malcolm X's ideology differed most fundamentally from Martin Luther King Jr's in that Malcolm X:

options.label: A
options.text: Opposed all forms of political participation by African Americans

options.label: B
options.text: Rejected racial integration in favour of Black separatism and self-determination

options.label: C
options.text: Argued that African Americans should emigrate to Africa permanently

options.label: D
options.text: Supported close cooperation with the Democratic Party to achieve civil rights legislation

markScheme: B — Malcolm X, influenced by Nation of Islam teachings, rejected integration as a goal, arguing instead for Black separatism, the right to self-defence, and Black pride. King sought integration into mainstream American society through nonviolent direct action.

prompt: The Nazi-Soviet Non-Aggression Pact of August 1939 contained a secret protocol that:

options.label: A
options.text: Agreed to a joint German-Soviet invasion of France

options.label: B
options.text: Divided Eastern Europe into German and Soviet spheres of influence

options.label: C
options.text: Guaranteed Soviet neutrality in any war between Germany and Britain

options.label: D
options.text: Established a full military alliance obligating mutual defence if either power was attacked

markScheme: B — The secret protocol divided Poland between Germany and the USSR and assigned Finland, Estonia, and Latvia to the Soviet sphere. This enabled Germany to invade Poland on 1 September 1939 without risking a two-front war.

prompt: Describe the significance of the Montgomery Bus Boycott (1955–1956) for the development of the US Civil Rights Movement.
markScheme:
• Rosa Parks arrested on 1 December 1955 for refusing to give up her seat — provides context [1]
• 381-day boycott organised by Montgomery Improvement Association (MIA) under Martin Luther King Jr [1]
• Economic impact: African Americans composed 75% of bus riders; sustained loss of revenue pressured city [1]
• Supreme Court ruling in Browder v Gayle (November 1956) declared bus segregation unconstitutional [1]
• Elevated King to national prominence; demonstrated power of mass economic protest [1]
• Established SCLC model of church-based nonviolent organisation; inspired subsequent campaigns [1]
Max 6 marks

prompt: Explain the causes of Japanese expansion in Asia between 1931 and 1937.
markScheme:
Economic causes:
• Japan lacked domestic raw materials (oil, iron ore, rubber) — imperial expansion offered access to these resources [1]
• Great Depression (1929) devastated Japan's export economy and caused severe domestic political instability [1]

Political/military causes:
• Rise of ultranationalist and militarist factions; Kwantung Army acted autonomously of civilian government in staging Mukden Incident [1]
• Civilian government too weak to control military; assassinations (e.g. Prime Minister Inukai, 1932) eliminated moderate opposition [1]
• Ideology of pan-Asian leadership ('Hakko Ichiu' — 'eight corners of the world under one roof') and racial destiny [1]

International context:
• League of Nations' failure — Lytton Commission report delayed; no sanctions imposed; Japan simply withdrew from League in 1933 [1]
• Western powers distracted by Depression and European instability; US isolationism limited response [1]
• Success of Manchukuo puppet state (1932) emboldened further ambitions in northern China [1]
Max 8 marks

prompt: Compare and contrast the methods used by Martin Luther King Jr and Malcolm X to advance African American civil rights in the 1960s.
markScheme:
Similarities:
• Both challenged white supremacy and the systematic inequality faced by African Americans [1]
• Both were powerful public orators who galvanised large followings [1]
• Both evolved in their views: King became more radical (Poor People's Campaign, anti-Vietnam); Malcolm X moderated after his pilgrimage to Mecca [1]

Differences — Methods:
• King: nonviolent direct action — sit-ins, marches, boycotts modelled on Gandhian satyagraha; sought to provoke moral crisis [1]
• Malcolm X: advocated self-defence ('by any means necessary'); rejected nonviolence as disarming Black people [1]

Differences — Goals:
• King sought racial integration within American society; Malcolm X (especially early) sought Black separatism and self-sufficiency [1]
• King worked with white liberals and the federal government; Malcolm X built independent Black institutions [1]

Broader significance:
• Their differences reflected deeper divisions in Black America between integration and separatism, and between reform and revolution [1]
Max 8 marks

prompt: Explain why Britain and France pursued a policy of appeasement towards Germany between 1936 and 1938.
markScheme:
• Deep public and political fear of another catastrophic war after WWI — anti-war sentiment dominated British opinion [1]
• Belief that some German grievances under Versailles were legitimate — 'revision not revolution' [1]
• British military unpreparedness — RAF rearmament not complete; army not ready for continental war until 1939 at earliest [1]
• Economic constraints of the Great Depression limited funding for rapid rearmament [1]
• Ideological fear of communism — Hitler seen by some as a bulwark against Soviet expansion [1]
• Chamberlain's genuine belief in personal diplomacy; misjudged Hitler as a rational statesman whose ambitions could be satisfied [1]
Max 6 marks

prompt: Describe the role of the Freedom Rides of 1961 in challenging racial segregation in the American South.
markScheme:
• CORE and SNCC organised interracial groups to ride interstate buses into the South to test enforcement of Boynton v Virginia (1960) ruling [1]
• First riders attacked in Anniston and Birmingham, Alabama; one bus firebombed; riders beaten by white mobs [1]
• Kennedy administration initially pressured organisers to halt but was ultimately compelled to intervene [1]
• Attorney General Robert Kennedy negotiated with Interstate Commerce Commission — new desegregation regulations for interstate travel facilities issued November 1961 [1]
• Media images of burning bus and beaten riders generated national and international outrage, embarrassing the US government [1]
• Demonstrated willingness of activists to risk serious violence for direct action; expanded SNCC's membership and radicalised the movement [1]
Max 6 marks

prompt: "The Civil Rights Act of 1964 was the most significant achievement of the US Civil Rights Movement." To what extent do you agree with this assessment?
markScheme:
Use IB Paper 2 markbands (0–15). Level 5 (13–15): clear analytical argument, specific evidence, evaluates multiple factors, substantiated conclusion.

Arguments FOR:
• Outlawed discrimination based on race, colour, religion, sex or national origin in employment (Title VII) and public accommodations (Title II)
• Created EEOC as enforcement mechanism
• Gave federal government power to withhold funds from discriminatory programmes
• Culminated years of protest: sit-ins, Freedom Rides, Birmingham campaign, March on Washington

Arguments AGAINST / alternative achievements:
• Voting Rights Act (1965) arguably more transformative — Black voter registration in Mississippi rose from 6.7% to 59.8% within three years
• Brown v Board (1954) provided constitutional foundation: overturned Plessy v Ferguson, delegitimised Jim Crow
• Montgomery Bus Boycott demonstrated power of mass economic direct action
• Limitations: Civil Rights Act did not address housing discrimination (Fair Housing Act 1968), structural poverty, or Northern de facto segregation
• Black Power movement argued legal equality was insufficient without economic transformation

Conclusion must weigh evidence and arrive at a clear, substantiated judgement.
exemplarAnswer:
The Civil Rights Act of 1964 represented a landmark legislative achievement, dismantling the legal infrastructure of Jim Crow and creating federal enforcement mechanisms absent since Reconstruction. Its breadth — public accommodations, employment, and federal funding — distinguished it from weaker earlier acts (1957, 1960). Yet to call it the single most significant achievement requires careful qualification.

The Act emerged directly from sustained mass protest. The Birmingham campaign of 1963, in which Bull Connor deployed fire hoses and police dogs against peaceful demonstrators, generated national outrage that compelled President Kennedy to propose sweeping legislation. The March on Washington in August 1963 — 250,000 participants, King's 'I Have a Dream' address — sustained political pressure that President Johnson channelled into the Act's passage. In this sense, the Act validated nonviolent direct action as a strategy.

Nevertheless, the Voting Rights Act of 1965 may claim equal or greater significance. By eliminating literacy tests and authorising federal oversight of elections, it translated formal civil rights into real political power. Mississippi's Black voter registration rose from 6.7% to 59.8% within three years. The ability to elect representatives and hold officials accountable arguably underpinned the durability of all other gains.

Equally foundational was Brown v Board of Education (1954). By overturning 'separate but equal' and reframing segregation as unconstitutional, Brown provided the legal and moral architecture on which all subsequent protest was built. Without it, the Civil Rights Act's scope would have been narrower.

The Act's limitations further complicate the assessment. It left de facto Northern segregation, housing discrimination, and structural poverty largely untouched. The Watts riots (1965) and subsequent urban unrest demonstrated that formal legal equality was insufficient. Critics from the Black Power movement — Stokely Carmichael, Malcolm X — argued that without economic power and cultural self-determination, civil rights legislation changed only the surface of American racism.

In conclusion, the Civil Rights Act of 1964 was indispensable, but not uniquely 'most significant'. The Voting Rights Act produced more measurable democratic transformation; Brown provided the constitutional foundation; and the Act's own inadequacies reveal how much remained unfinished. Significance is properly understood as cumulative — each achievement building on and requiring the others.

prompt: Evaluate the role of appeasement in causing the Second World War in Europe.
markScheme:
Use IB Paper 2 markbands (0–15).

Appeasement as cause:
• Munich 1938 confirmed Hitler's conviction that Western powers lacked will to fight — emboldened him to occupy all of Czechoslovakia in March 1939
• Each concession (Rhineland 1936, Anschluss March 1938, Sudetenland September 1938) raised Hitler's demands
• Surrendering Czech defences and Skoda armaments industry materially weakened Allied strategic position
• Failed to build collective security; excluded USSR, pushing Stalin toward the Nazi-Soviet Pact
• Chamberlain's 'peace in our time' gave Hitler time to complete rearmament on his terms

Appeasement not the primary cause:
• Hitler's expansionist ideology (Mein Kampf, Hossbach Memorandum 1937) suggests war was planned regardless
• A.J.P. Taylor argued appeasement was rational given Allied unpreparedness; war might have been worse in 1938
• Nazi-Soviet Pact (August 1939) enabled invasion of Poland — Soviet foreign policy also a cause
• American isolationism and weakness of League of Nations were structural factors beyond British/French control
• Some historians argue appeasement bought Britain crucial time for RAF rearmament (Battle of Britain, 1940)

Level 5 (13–15): sustained analytical evaluation, engages with historiographical debate, clear and well-supported conclusion on relative weight of appeasement.

prompt: Compare and contrast Japanese and German expansion in the 1930s, with reference to their methods, motivations, and the international responses they provoked.
markScheme:
Use IB Paper 3 markbands (0–15).

Similarities:
• Both driven by economic needs and imperial ideology — Japan needed raw materials; Germany pursued Lebensraum [1–2]
• Both exploited weaknesses of the international system — League of Nations failed to stop either power [1]
• Both used military force incrementally, testing Western resolve before escalating [1]
• Both driven by extreme nationalist ideology — Japanese ultramilitarism and pan-Asianism; German National Socialism [1]
• Both established puppet or client states (Manchukuo; Bohemia-Moravia protectorate) [1]

Differences:
• Germany's expansion was racially ideological (Aryan supremacy, anti-Semitism); Japanese expansion framed in pan-Asian, anti-colonial terms [1]
• German targets were European neighbours with ethnic German populations; Japan expanded into China and Southeast Asia [1]
• German expansion followed Hitler's deliberate sequence; Japanese military often acted autonomously of civilian government [1]
• Western powers ultimately declared war on Germany over Poland; Japan faced less organised Western resistance until Pearl Harbor (1941) [1]
• Scale of international reaction: League condemned Japan over Manchuria but imposed no effective sanctions; Munich showed even greater Western passivity toward Germany [1]

Level 5 (13–15): sophisticated comparison using specific evidence from both cases; reaches clear analytical conclusion about similarities and differences.

prompt: To what extent was nonviolent direct action the most important factor in achieving civil rights legislation in the United States by 1965?
markScheme:
Use IB Paper 2 markbands (0–15).

Nonviolent direct action as key factor:
• Montgomery Boycott (1955–56), sit-ins (1960), Freedom Rides (1961) demonstrated mass mobilisation and economic pressure
• Birmingham campaign (1963): televised police brutality generated national outrage and forced Kennedy to act
• March on Washington (August 1963): sustained congressional pressure for Civil Rights Act
• Selma-to-Montgomery marches (March 1965): 'Bloody Sunday' broadcast nationally; directly triggered Voting Rights Act
• King's moral authority and rhetoric gave movement credibility with white moderates and federal government

Other significant factors:
• Cold War context — racial inequality embarrassed US in ideological battle with USSR, especially in newly independent African and Asian nations
• Legal strategy — NAACP's court litigation (Brown v Board, Browder v Gayle) changed constitutional landscape
• Federal government role — Kennedy and especially Johnson used presidential authority and personal commitment
• Economic factors — Black consumer power; Northern Black vote becoming electorally significant
• Pressure from more radical movements (SNCC radicalisation, Black Power) created incentive for moderates to pass legislation quickly
• International context — decolonisation made US racial practices a global embarrassment

Level 5 (13–15): evaluates multiple factors with analytical depth, uses specific evidence, argues clearly for relative importance of nonviolent direct action vs other factors, substantiated conclusion.
